questions.question:
A bag has r red balls and b black balls. All balls are identical except for their colours. In a trial, a ball is randomly drawn from the bag, its colour is noted and the ball is placed back into the bag along with another ball of the same colour. Note that the number of balls in the bag will increase by one after each trial. A sequence of four such trials is conducted. What is the probability of drawing a red ball in the fourth trial?

A. r / (r + b)
B. (r + 3) / (r + b + 3)
C. (r + b + 3) / r
D. r / (r + b + 3)

understand.plain: In a Polya urn, at each step you draw a ball and return it along with a new ball of the same colour. Despite the bag growing, the probability of drawing red at any trial always equals the initial fraction r/(r+b). This is because the Polya urn model forms a martingale: the expected proportion of red balls never changes. The fourth trial is no different from the first — the answer is simply r/(r+b).

keywords.explain: A random process where drawn balls are returned along with an extra ball of the same colour; the proportion of each colour is a martingale.
keywords.example: Bag starts with 1 red and 1 black. P(red on any trial) = 1/2 always.

keywords.explain: A sequence where the expected future value equals the current value; here the expected fraction of red balls stays constant.
keywords.example: E[red fraction after k trials] = r/(r+b) regardless of k.

keywords.explain: P(red on trial k) is computed by summing over all possible outcomes of previous trials, which collapses to r/(r+b).
keywords.example: P(red on trial 2) = P(RR)/(r+b+1) + P(BR)*r/(r+b+1) simplifies to r/(r+b).

understand.visual_alt: A bag being filled after each trial, yet the red fraction stays at r/(r+b).

given.aim: Find P(red on 4th trial) given Polya urn starting with r red and b black balls.

terms.example: r = 2 means 2 red balls at start
b = 3 means 3 black balls at start
terms.connects: Together with r, sets r+b as total initial count

terms.meaning: Probability of drawing red on the k-th trial
terms.connects: Target quantity; equals r/(r+b) for all k by martingale property

given.plan: Use the Polya urn martingale property: at any trial k, P(red) = r/(r+b). Verify by computing P(red on trial 2) via total probability and confirm the pattern persists.

steps.title: Compute P(red on trial 2) by total probability
steps.formula_raw: P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)
steps.note: The r+1 and b terms combine to cancel the denominator increment, confirming the martingale property.

steps.title: Generalise: P(red on trial k) = r/(r+b) for all k
steps.formula_raw: P(red on trial k) = r / (r + b)
steps.apply: By induction the same cancellation occurs at every step. For trial 4, P(red) = r/(r+b), matching option A.
steps.note: This result is independent of all previous outcomes — the Polya urn probability of red is always the initial fraction.

solution.result: P(red on 4th trial) = r / (r + b)   [Option A]

questions.formula_note: The Polya urn martingale means the initial fraction r/(r+b) is the answer for every trial, not just the first.

questions.question:
Let A and B be two events in a probability space with P(A) = 0.3, P(B) = 0.5, and P(A | B) = 0.1. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. The two events A and B are independent and P(A | B) = 0.3 and P(B | A) = 0.5, respectively.
B. P(A union B) = 0.75
C. P(A^c | B) = 0.9, where A^c is the complement of the event A
D. P(A | B^c) = P(A | B), where A^c and B^c are the complements of the events A and B respectively

understand.plain: Given P(A)=0.3, P(B)=0.5, P(A|B)=0.1, we first find P(A and B) = P(A|B)*P(B) = 0.05. Since P(A)*P(B)=0.15 != 0.05, the events are NOT independent. P(A union B) = 0.3 + 0.5 - 0.05 = 0.75. P(A^c|B) = 1 - P(A|B) = 0.9. P(A|B^c) = P(A and B^c)/P(B^c) = 0.25/0.5 = 0.5 != 0.1, so D is false.

keywords.explain: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B); the probability of A given that B has occurred.
keywords.example: P(A|B)=0.1 means knowing B occurred, A has only 10% chance.

keywords.explain: Events A and B are independent iff P(A and B) = P(A)*P(B), equivalently iff P(A|B) = P(A).
keywords.example: Here P(A|B)=0.1 != 0.3=P(A), so A and B are NOT independent.

keywords.explain: P(A^c|B) = 1 - P(A|B) because conditioning on B, the conditional probabilities of A and A^c sum to 1.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing A and B with small overlap P(A and B)=0.05.

given.aim: Determine which statements about A and B are TRUE given P(A)=0.3, P(B)=0.5, P(A|B)=0.1.

terms.meaning: Joint probability of both A and B occurring
terms.connects: Key derived quantity used in all four statements

terms.meaning: Probability that at least one of A or B occurs

given.plan: Derive P(A and B), check independence, compute P(A union B), P(A^c|B), and P(A|B^c) to evaluate each statement.

steps.title: Find P(A and B)
steps.formula_raw: P(A and B) = P(A | B) * P(B)
steps.note: This is the key derived value for all subsequent checks.

steps.title: Check Statement A: independence
steps.formula_raw: Independent iff P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B)
steps.apply: P(A)*P(B) = 0.3*0.5 = 0.15 != 0.05 = P(A and B). NOT independent. Statement A is FALSE.

steps.title: Check Statement B: P(A union B)
steps.formula_raw: P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
steps.apply: P(A union B) = 0.3 + 0.5 - 0.05 = 0.75. Statement B is TRUE.

steps.title: Check Statement C: P(A^c | B)
steps.note: Conditional probabilities given B sum to 1, so complement rule applies directly.

steps.title: Check Statement D: P(A | B^c)
steps.formula_raw: P(A | B^c) = P(A and B^c) / P(B^c)
steps.apply: P(A and B^c) = P(A) - P(A and B) = 0.3 - 0.05 = 0.25. P(B^c) = 0.5. P(A|B^c) = 0.25/0.5 = 0.5 != 0.1 = P(A|B). Statement D is FALSE.
steps.note: D would be true only if A and B were independent, which they are not.

solution.result: Statements B and C are TRUE.

questions.formula_note: Inclusion-exclusion and the complement rule for conditional probabilities are the core tools here.

questions.question:
When six unbiased dice are rolled simultaneously, the probability of getting all distinct numbers is

A. 1/6
B. 5/324
C. 1/54
D. 5/1296

understand.plain: Six dice are rolled and we want all six to show different numbers (i.e., each of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 exactly once). The total number of outcomes is 6^6. The number of outcomes where all six numbers are distinct is 6! (arrange 1-6 among the 6 dice). The probability is 6! / 6^6 = 720 / 46656 = 5/324.

keywords.explain: The set of all possible outcomes when six dice are rolled; each die shows 1-6 independently.
Outcomes where all six dice show distinct values; since there are only 6 distinct values and 6 dice, each value appears exactly once.
When all outcomes are equally likely, probability = favorable / total.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><rect x='85' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='115' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><rect x='160' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='190' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><rect x='235' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='265' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><rect x='310' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><rect x='385' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='415' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>6</text><text x='240' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>All 6 dice show distinct values: 6! arrangements out of 6^6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Six dice each showing a different number from 1 to 6.

given.aim: Find the probability that six dice rolled simultaneously all show different numbers.

terms.meaning: Total number of equally likely outcomes when 6 dice are rolled
Number of ways to assign distinct values 1-6 to 6 dice (all permutations)

given.plan: Count total outcomes (6^6), count favorable outcomes (all distinct = 6!), divide and simplify.

steps.title: Count total outcomes
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: Each of 6 dice has 6 faces. Total = 6 x 6 x 6 x 6 x 6 x 6 = 6^6 = 46656.
steps.note: Dice rolls are independent, so multiply.

steps.title: Count favorable outcomes (all distinct)
steps.apply: We need each die to show a different number from {1,...,6}. The first die can be any of 6 values, second any of 5 remaining, etc. Favorable = 6 x 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 6! = 720.
steps.note: Exactly P(6,6) = 6! since all 6 dice must show all 6 distinct values.

questions.formula_note: The favorable count is 6! (permutations of 6 distinct values across 6 dice), divided by 6^6 total outcomes.

questions.question: A box contains 5 coins: 4 regular coins and 1 fake coin. When a regular coin is tossed, the probability P(head) = 0.5, and for a fake coin, P(head) = 1. You pick a coin at random and toss it twice, and get two heads. The probability that the coin you have chosen is the fake coin is __________. (rounded off to two decimal places)

understand.plain: You randomly pick one of 5 coins (4 fair, 1 always-heads). After tossing twice and getting HH, you update belief using Bayes theorem. The fake coin always gives HH (probability 1), while a fair coin gives HH with probability 0.25. The posterior probability of having the fake coin is (1/5 * 1) / (1/5 * 1 + 4/5 * 1/4) = 0.2 / 0.4 = 0.5.

keywords.explain: Updates prior probability using observed evidence: P(cause|effect) = P(effect|cause)*P(cause) / P(effect).
Initial probability before observing evidence; P(fake) = 1/5, P(regular) = 4/5.
keywords.example: Without any toss data, each coin is equally likely to be chosen.
The fake coin makes HH certain; a fair coin makes HH only 25% likely.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 150'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Fake coin (1 of 5)</text><text x='100' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P(HH) = 1</text><rect x='230' y='10' width='180' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='320' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Regular coin (4 of 5)</text><text x='320' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P(HH) = 0.25</text><text x='210' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P(fake|HH) = 0.2 / 0.4 = 0.5</text><text x='210' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Bayes: weight by prior x likelihood</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two branches from the coin box: fake (P=1/5, P(HH)=1) and regular (P=4/5, P(HH)=0.25); posterior for fake given HH is 0.5.

given.aim: Find P(fake coin | HH) using Bayes theorem.

terms.meaning: Prior probability of picking the fake coin
Probability of two heads given fake coin
terms.example: Fake coin always shows heads: P(HH|fake) = 1*1 = 1
terms.connects: Likelihood for the fake coin branch

terms.meaning: Probability of two heads given a regular coin
terms.connects: Likelihood for the regular coin branch

given.plan: Apply Bayes theorem: compute P(HH) via total probability using both coin types, then divide P(HH and fake) by P(HH).

steps.title: Compute total probability of observing HH
steps.note: Both branches contribute equally to P(HH) = 0.4.

questions.formula_note: Bayes theorem: the posterior equals the likelihood times the prior divided by the total probability of the evidence.

questions.question:
A random experiment consists of throwing 10 fair dice, each die having six faces numbered 1 to 6. An event A represents the set of all outcomes where at least one of the dice shows a 1. Then P(A) =

A. 0
B. (1/6)^10
C. 1 - (5/6)^10
D. (5/6)^10

understand.plain: Event A = at least one die shows 1. It is easiest to use the complement: P(A^c) = P(no die shows 1) = (5/6)^10, since each die independently shows 1 with probability 1/6. Then P(A) = 1 - (5/6)^10. Numerically this is approximately 0.8385, confirming C is correct.

keywords.explain: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none), often simpler than counting directly.
keywords.example: P(at least one 1 in 10 dice) = 1 - P(all dice show 2-6).

keywords.explain: Each die roll is independent, so P(all dice avoid 1) = product of individual probabilities.
A single die shows one of {2,3,4,5,6} with probability 5/6.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 460 130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='15' width='200' height='60' rx='8' class='no' opacity='0.25'/><rect x='10' y='15' width='200' height='60' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='110' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A^c: no die shows 1</text><text x='110' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P = (5/6)^10 ≈ 0.162</text><rect x='250' y='15' width='200' height='60' rx='8' class='ok' opacity='0.25'/><rect x='250' y='15' width='200' height='60' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='350' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A: at least one die = 1</text><text x='350' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P = 1 - (5/6)^10 ≈ 0.838</text><text x='230' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P(A) + P(A^c) = 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two complementary regions: A^c (no die shows 1, probability (5/6)^10) and A (at least one die shows 1, probability 1-(5/6)^10).

given.aim: Find P(A) where A = at least one die shows 1 when 10 fair dice are rolled.

terms.meaning: Ten independent fair dice are rolled simultaneously
terms.example: Each die independently shows 1-6 with equal probability 1/6
terms.connects: Sets up the independence used in the complement calculation

terms.meaning: Probability a single die shows 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6
terms.connects: Base probability for the complement product

terms.meaning: Complement of A: none of the 10 dice shows 1

given.plan: Use the complement: P(at least one 1) = 1 - P(no die shows 1). P(no die shows 1) = (5/6)^10 by independence.

steps.title: Identify complement event A^c
steps.apply: A^c = event that none of the 10 dice shows 1. P(A) = 1 - P(A^c).
steps.note: Using complement avoids summing over all ways exactly 1, 2, ..., 10 dice show 1.

steps.title: Compute P(A^c) using independence
steps.formula_raw: P(all independent events) = P(E_1) * P(E_2) * ... * P(E_n)
steps.apply: Each die avoids 1 with probability 5/6. P(A^c) = (5/6)^10.
steps.note: Independence of dice is crucial: no single die affects the others.

questions.formula_note: Complement + independence: P(at least one 1) = 1 - (5/6)^10, where (5/6)^10 is the probability all dice avoid 1.
